name: Robinhood
overview:
Robinhood is a commission-free stock trading platform that allows retail investors to buy and sell stocks, ETFs,
and options. The system must display real-time stock prices, process trade orders with strong consistency,
maintain accurate account balances and positions, and comply with financial regulations (SEC, FINRA). The
system must handle extreme load during market volatility (e.g., meme stock events) while maintaining
correctness.

inScope: Display real-time stock quotes (price, volume, change)
Maintain accurate portfolio positions and cash balance

items: Real-time quote updates with <1 second latency during market hours
Order submission latency <200ms; order fill notification within seconds of broker confirmation
99.99% availability during market hours (9:30 AM–4:00 PM ET) — downtime prevents trading
Strong consistency for account balances and positions — no overselling or negative balances
Handle 10x traffic spikes during high-volatility events (meme stocks, earnings)

entities.name: Order
entities.schema:
{
  order_id: UUID,
  user_id: string,
  symbol: string,
  order_type: enum(MARKET|LIMIT|STOP),
  side: enum(BUY|SELL),

  quantity: decimal,
  limit_price: decimal (nullable),
  status: enum(PENDING|SUBMITTED|FILLED|PARTIAL|CANCELLED|REJECTED),
  filled_quantity: decimal,
  avg_fill_price: decimal,
  submitted_at: timestamp
}

entities.name: Position
entities.schema:
{
  user_id: string,
  symbol: string,
  quantity: decimal,
  avg_cost: decimal,
  current_value: decimal
}

approaches.title: Polling Market Data API
approaches.description: Poll a market data provider every second for all tracked symbols. At 10k symbols × 1 req/sec = 10k API calls/sec. Market data APIs charge per call. Also, 1-second polling misses intra-second price movements.
approaches.tradeoff: Expensive; misses intra-second movements; high API call volume.

approaches.title: WebSocket Feed from Market Data Provider
approaches.description: Subscribe to a WebSocket feed from a market data provider (Polygon.io, IEX Cloud). Receive real-time tick data for all symbols. Sub-second price updates. But the feed must be distributed to millions of users — a single WebSocket connection cannot fan out to 10M users.
approaches.tradeoff: Fan-out from single feed to 10M users requires additional infrastructure.

approaches.title: Market Data Feed + Redis Pub/Sub Fan-out
approaches.description: Subscribe to market data WebSocket feed. Publish price updates to Redis pub/sub channels (one per symbol). Quote servers subscribe to relevant channels and push to connected users. Users subscribe to symbols they're watching. Quote servers maintain WebSocket connections with users and push updates only for subscribed symbols. Price updates are also stored in Redis (latest price per symbol) for REST API queries.
approaches.tradeoff: Redis pub/sub doesn't persist messages; users who miss an update must poll for current price.

approaches.title: Direct Broker API Call
approaches.description: Submit orders directly to the broker-dealer API synchronously. Broker API latency (100-500ms) blocks the user request. If the broker is slow, the entire order flow is slow.
approaches.tradeoff: Broker latency blocks user; tight coupling to broker availability.

approaches.title: Async Order Queue with Status Polling
approaches.description: Submit order to internal queue (Kafka). Order Service processes asynchronously, submits to broker, and updates order status. User receives order_id immediately. Order status is updated asynchronously. WebSocket push notifies user when order is filled. Pre-validation (sufficient balance, market hours) is synchronous before queuing.
approaches.tradeoff: Async processing means user doesn't know immediately if order was accepted by broker.

components.name: Quote Service
components.responsibility: Subscribes to market data feed; publishes to Redis pub/sub; serves

components.name: Order Service
components.responsibility: Validates orders; deducts buying power; submits to broker via Kafka

components.name: Broker Integration
components.responsibility: Consumes Kafka; submits orders to broker-dealer API; processes fill notifications

components.name: Portfolio Service
components.responsibility: Maintains positions and cash balances; updated on order fills

components.name: Market Data Feed
components.responsibility: Real-time tick data from Polygon.io/IEX Cloud via WebSocket

components.name: Redis
components.responsibility: Latest prices per symbol; pub/sub channels for real-time distribution

components.name: Order DB (PostgreSQL)
components.responsibility: Stores all orders; ACID transactions for balance updates

components.name: WebSocket Gateway
components.responsibility: Maintains user connections; pushes quote updates and order status

questions.title: 2. Order Fill Processing
questions.content:
When the broker fills an order, it sends a fill notification via webhook or WebSocket. The Broker Integration
service processes the fill: (1) Update order status to FILLED, (2) Update portfolio positions (add shares for buy,
remove for sell), (3) Update cash balance (deduct for buy, add for sell), (4) Release any remaining reservation.
All updates are in a single PostgreSQL transaction.

questions.title: 3. Market Hours and Circuit Breakers
questions.content:
Orders can only be submitted during market hours (9:30 AM - 4:00 PM ET for US markets). The Order Service
checks market hours before accepting orders. During extreme volatility, exchanges implement circuit breakers
(trading halts). The system monitors exchange status via the market data feed and rejects orders during halts.
Extended hours trading (pre-market and after-hours) is supported with different order types.

questions.title: 4. Quote Throttling for Popular Stocks
questions.content:
During meme stock events (e.g., GameStop), a single stock can receive millions of quote subscriptions. The
Quote Service throttles updates for heavily subscribed symbols: instead of pushing every tick, it batches
updates and pushes every 100ms. This reduces WebSocket message volume by 10x during peak events while
maintaining near-real-time price visibility.

questions.title: 5. Regulatory Compliance
questions.content:
Robinhood must comply with SEC and FINRA regulations: (1) Best execution: orders must be routed to the
venue offering the best price, (2) Pattern Day Trader (PDT) rule: users with <$25k equity cannot make >3 day
trades in 5 days, (3) Wash sale rule: selling at a loss and rebuying within 30 days disallows the tax deduction.
The system enforces PDT rules in the Order Service and tracks wash sales for tax reporting.

keyFlows: ① Market data feed → Quote Svc → Redis pub/sub → WebSocket → User
② User places order → Order API (validate balance) → Kafka
③ Broker Integration consumes Kafka → submits to Broker API
④ Broker fills order → fill notification → update Portfolio + Order status
⑤ WebSocket push: order filled notification to user

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study market microstructure: how stock exchanges work (order books, matching engines),
the difference between market orders and limit orders, and how price discovery happens.
Expert Note: Learn about broker-dealer regulations: SEC Rule 15c3-3 (customer protection rule), FINRA
margin requirements, and how payment for order flow (PFOF) works.
Expert Note: Understand the FIX protocol: the standard messaging format for financial transactions, how
order routing works between broker-dealers and exchanges.
Expert Note: Study the Pattern Day Trader rule: the $25k equity requirement, how day trades are counted,
and the implications for retail trading platforms.
Expert Note: Learn about market data distribution: how exchanges distribute real-time data via SIP
(Securities Information Processor) and direct feeds, and the latency differences.
